reviews.author: Dan Williams
reviews.summary: Reviewer Dan Williams suggested that instead of making the interface more generic, the kernel should clearly indicate which methods are available and allow for a deprecation period before removing native support. He proposed adding a capability bit or similar mechanism to make it clear that native support might be removed in the future.
reviews.raw_body:
Right, if the protocol on day one is "user space must ask which method is available", then userspace can not be surprised when one option disappears. So to give time for the bpf approach to mature the kernel can do something like "famfs and bpf  mapping support are available". In some future kernel the famfs native option disappears after a deprecation period. 

When folks ask 10 years from now why this ever supported optionality the explanation is "oh because famfs enjoyed first mover advantage to prove out fs semantics layered on dax devices", or "turns out there are some cases where bpf is not fast enough but it still stops the proliferation of more in kernel mapping implementations".

Something like FUSE_DAX_FMAP is always available but the backend to that is optionally native vs bpf. ...or some other arrangement to make it clear that native might be gone someday.

reviews.author: Darrick Wong
reviews.summary:
Reviewer Darrick Wong expressed concern that the patch could break fuse servers if it removes GET_FMAP in favor of IOMAP_BEGIN without also updating libfuse to translate ->get_fmap implementations into ->iomap_begin, which would require dealing with vendored libfuse versions.

Reviewer Darrick Wong noted that famfs requires the ability to express formulaic meta-mapping, which cannot be efficiently achieved using traditional iomap parlance and individual iomaps; he believes there's no good reason to remove GET_FMAP at this time.

Reviewer Darrick Wong suggested a different approach to resolving the interface generality issue by merging fuse-iomap after famfs and extending the existing fuse-iomap-bpf prototype to allow per-mount and per-inode iomap bpf ops. This would enable thorough analysis of performance characteristics for various approaches, including using GET_FMAP as-is, uploading raw iomaps, or uploading custom bpf programs.

reviewer emphasized the importance of testing and gathering user feedback before making changes to the interface, suggesting that shipping the new mechanism to users is necessary for determining the best approach
reviews.raw_body:
So don't break fuse servers.  If you wanted to (say) get rid of
GET_FMAP in favor of IOMAP_BEGIN, you could alter libfuse to translate a
fuse server's ->get_fmap implementation into the equivalent
->iomap_begin, and eventually the kernel can stop making GET_FMAP calls
to userspace.

The trouble here is that I've also seen half a dozen projects vendoring
libfuse so that's a nightmare that will have to be dealt with.  But
maybe that doesn't even matter, because...

---

...the memory interleaving is a rather interesting quality of famfs.
There's no good way to express a formulaic meta-mapping in traditional
iomap parlance, and famfs needs that to interleave across memory
controllers/dimm boxen/whatever.  Throwing individual iomaps at the
kernel is a very inefficient way to do that.  So I don't think there's a
good reason to get rid of GET_FMAP at this time...

---

...however the strongest case (IMO) would be if (having merged famfs) we
then merge fuse-iomap after famfs.  Then we extend the existing
fuse-iomap-bpf prototype to allow per-mount and per-inode iomap bpf ops.
That enables us to analyze thoroughly the performance characteristics of:

a) Using GET_FMAP as-is

b) Uploading raw iomaps (HA)

c) Uploading a single bpf program to make iomaps, exchanging fmap-style
mapping data into a bpf map, and having the single bpf program walk
through the map

d) Uploading a custom bpf program per famfs file to make iomaps.  No
bpfmap required, but the setup and compilation are now much more complex

Then we'll finally know which approach is the best, having broken the
Gordian Knot of how to merge famfs and fuse-iomap.

If we decide that (c) or (d) are actually better, then guess what?  To
get any of the iomap functionality, you have to set an inode flag, and
that (FUSE_CAP_FAMFS && FUSE_CAP_IOMAP && FUSE_ATTR_IOMAP) is the signal
for "don't call GET_FMAP".  FUSE_CAP_FAMFS && (!FUSE_CAP_IOMAP ||
!FUSE_ATTR_IOMAP) means "call GET_FMAP".

Yes, we burn a couple of fuse command values to find out, but that's all.

(TBH I still dislike GET_DAXDEV, that really should just be another
application of backing files, and the backing file id gets passed to
GET_FMAP.)

What do you all think of doing that?

---

Yes.  We're not *capable* of determining the best mechanism unless we
can start shipping these things to users to get their feedback.  Only
then can we iterate and make real improvements.

reviews.author: Joanne Koong
reviews.summary:
Reviewer Joanne Koong expressed concern that relying on capability bits for deprecation may not be sufficient, as it does not address the issue of breaking users who are on older versions of libfuse if they upgrade to a newer kernel. She also questioned the deprecation period and process, asking what timeframe is required before a feature can be fully removed.

Reviewer Joanne Koong suggested making the interleaving part of the interface generic by adding an interleave descriptor to the user API, citing examples from other storage technologies such as RAID-0 and md-stripe.

Reviewer Joanne Koong expressed concerns that the GET_FMAP interface is too specific and may not be suitable for other interleaved or striped layouts, requesting it to be made more generic.
reviews.raw_body:
I don't think it's this simple. We can't assume libfuse is the only
way servers talk to the kernel. Some servers use the /dev/fuse
interface directly. And, as I understand it, this would still break
users who are on older versions of libfuse if they upgrade to a newer
kernel.

My reason for pushing back isn't because I don't want this to work; I
just want to make sure that if we're going to rely on this as a safety
hatch, then we can actually do it.

Going back to what Dan said about using the capability bits for
deprecation, "In some future kernel the famfs native option disappears
after a deprecation period" - what does the deprecation period/process
look like? Do you have to wait a certain amount of time before it can
be fully removed or is it pretty immediate?

---

So could we make the interleaving part generic then? Striped /
interleaved layouts are used elsewhere (eg RAID-0, md-stripe, etc.) -
could we add a generic interleave descriptor to the uapi and use that
for what famfs needs?

---

To be completely honest, this is orthogonal to what I was hoping we
could discuss on this thread. My main concern is the GET_FMAP part.
Can we make it more generic to other interleaved/striped layouts?

Thanks,
Joanne

reviews.author: Joanne Koong
reviews.summary: Reviewer Joanne Koong questioned whether the interface is already generic and reusable for other dax-backed servers, despite being named famfs-specific, citing concerns that the naming conventions are implementation-specific.

reviews.author: Darrick Wong
reviews.summary:
Reviewer questioned the feasibility of making the interface more generic, citing concerns about performance overhead and the difficulty of finding a one-size-fits-all solution.

Reviewer Darrick Wong questioned whether a name change and capability bit could solve the issue of over-genericizing the interface, pointing out that md-raid presents a unified LBA address space, allowing filesystems to be unaware of translations underneath it.

Reviewer questioned the need for an overly generic interface, citing lack of evidence that it's actually needed and expressing concern that it would invite over-generalization.
reviews.raw_body:
That depends on how much gluecode you can stand up to redirect older
programs.

---

I doubt it.  md-raid presents a unified LBA address space, which means
that the filesystem doesn't have to know anything about whatever
translations might happen underneath it.  Even memory controllers
quietly take care of striping across DIMMs and whatnot.

Most filesystems that implement striping themselves don't restrict
themselves to monotonically increasing LBA ranges rotored across each
device like md-raid0 does.

But for whatever reason, pmem/dax don't have remapping layers like
md/dm so filesystems have to do that on their own if the hardware
doesn't do it for them.

---

"Generic"... do we even /have/ a second user?  I don't feel like we do.

--D

2026-04-16.patch_summary: This patch removes the requirement for FUSE_DAX_FMAP to be advertised in perpetuity, even if CONFIG_FUSE_DAX is disabled. It uses capability bits to control support for DAX, similar to cpuid capability bits. The goal is to prevent over-genericization of the interface and allow for more targeted solutions.
